title: Gamaṇāgamaṇo (Confession of Faults in Coming and Going)
summary: Gamaṇāgamaṇo is a short confession recited daily in which the practitioner asks pardon for any harm caused to living beings while walking to and from the place of worship or ritual. Going and coming on the road, one may unknowingly tread on or brush against tiny creatures, plants, seeds, water, earth, and other people; here the śrāvaka admits that, in moving about, the eight 'mothers of the doctrine' (five samitis and three guptis) were not kept perfectly, and so closes with the formula micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ — 'may that wrong deed be in vain.' It trains attentiveness (apramāda) and reverence for all life even in ordinary movement.
ritualPlacement: In the Daivasika (Devasi) pratikramaṇa, Gamaṇāgamaṇo follows the second Āvaśyaka unit — it comes after the kāyotsarga and Logassa, and is recited alongside the Jīvarāśi, the eighteen Pāpasthānaka, and the Dravya-Kṣetra-Kāla-Bhāva declaration, before the Karemi Bhante sāmāyika vow. It belongs to the cluster of confessional preliminaries that purify the practitioner of harm done while approaching the rite. The book attests its use in the Devasi pratikramaṇa (canonical order 7); like the other core preliminaries it is shared by reference into the other observances rather than reprinted, and where shared sūtras recur in Pakkhī/Cāumāsī/Sāṁvatsarī the standard pakkhī→cāumāsī→saṁvatsarī word-substitution applies (Cāumāsī and Sāṁvatsarī carry no independent text of their own).
authorAndSource: — (No individual author is named in the source book for this confession; it is a traditional liturgical formula of the Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha Devasi vidhi, given verbatim in પંચ પ્રતિક્રમણ સૂત્ર – વિધિ સહિત. Its doctrinal content — the six classes of embodied life and the eightfold pravacana-mātā — is drawn from the standard Jain canonical framework.)
structureArc: This sutra is a single continuous verse (v.1). It unfolds in three movements: first it names the occasion — 'while going and coming on the road' (mārgane viṣe jātāṁ-āvatāṁ); next it enumerates what may have been harmed or touched — the six jīva-classes (pṛthvīkāya, apkāya, teukāya, vāukāya, vanaspatikāya, traskāya) and concrete instances of them (green sprouts/flowers, earth, water, grain, cotton-seeds) together with contact (saṅghaṭṭa) with women or men (with the prescribed gender-swap: a woman says 'contact with men, etc.'); then it turns to the failure of conduct — that the eight pravacana-mātās (five samitis, three guptis) were not well kept in the śrāvaka's dharma, that breach (khaṇḍana) and injury (virādhanā) occurred — and seals all of it with the threefold admission by mind, speech, and body: micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ.
recurringImagery: The sutra's organizing motif is the aṣṭa-pravacana-mātā — the 'eight mothers of the doctrine' that nurture right conduct as a mother nurtures a child. They comprise the five samitis (careful conduct in action: īryā/walking, bhāṣā/speech, eṣaṇā/seeking food, ādāna-nikṣepaṇā/handling objects, pāriṣṭhāpanikā/disposal) and the three guptis (restraint of mind, speech, and body). Movement on the road is the test case for the first samiti, īryā-samiti (watchful walking), so the whole confession is an application of these eight 'mothers' to the simple act of going and coming. The second motif is the ṣaḍ-jīvanikāya — the six classes of embodied beings — which frames every Jain reflection on ahiṁsā: harm is possible to earth-bodied, water-bodied, fire-bodied, air-bodied, plant-bodied, and mobile beings alike.
keyThemes: The eight pravacana-mātās (five samitis, three guptis)
The six classes of living beings (ṣaḍ-jīvanikāya)
Threefold purity of mind, speech, and body

glossary.definition: 'Going and coming' — movement to and from the place of ritual, the occasion whose faults this sutra confesses.
The 'eight mothers of the doctrine': the five samitis plus the three guptis, which together nurture and protect right conduct.
Carefulness — the five rules of vigilant conduct (in walking, speech, seeking food, handling things, and disposal) that prevent harm to living beings.
Restraint or guarding — the threefold control of mind (mana), speech (vacana), and body (kāya).
Carefulness in walking, so as not to harm beings underfoot; the samiti most directly engaged by movement on the road.
The six classes of embodied life: earth-bodied (pṛthvīkāya), water-bodied (apkāya), fire-bodied (teukāya), air-bodied (vāukāya), plant-bodied (vanaspatikāya), and mobile (traskāya) beings.
Earth-bodied beings — life embodied in earth and minerals.
Water-bodied beings — life embodied in water.
Fire-bodied beings — life embodied in fire.
Air-bodied beings — life embodied in air/wind.
Plant-bodied beings — life embodied in vegetation.
Mobile beings — those able to move of their own accord (insects, animals, humans).
Contact or brushing-against; here, inadvertent physical touch of beings or persons while moving.
Injury to or violation of living beings or of one's vows.
Breaking or breach — failure to keep a rule of conduct intact.
'May my wrong deed be in vain' — the formula of confession and withdrawal of a transgression, by mind, speech, and body.
A lay follower (male; śrāvikā, female) who undertakes the lay vows of Jain conduct.

howToRead: A newcomer can read this simply as a daily apology for any tiny harm done while walking to worship, and an exercise in walking mindfully; the recurring terms (the six life-classes, the eight pravacana-mātās) are defined once in the glossary above so the verse need not re-explain them. Advanced practitioners should note the gender-swap rubric (a woman recites 'contact with men, etc.'), the precise mapping of road-movement onto īryā-samiti within the eightfold pravacana-mātā, and the threefold (mind/speech/body) sealing of the confession.

sources.title: Panch Pratikraman Sutra – Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha edition)
sources.consultedFor: Verbatim sutra text, Devasi ritual placement, and Anchalgachchha tradition framing.

sources.title: Samiti and Gupti (Aṣṭa Pravacana Mātā) — Jain conduct framework
sources.consultedFor: Names and definitions of the five samitis and three guptis.

notes: The source book gives no individual author for this confession; it is treated as a traditional formula of the Devasi vidhi. The verse is a single continuous prose-confession rather than a metrical gāthā, so 'verse 1' denotes the whole unit. Orthography in the book varies (e.g. અપ્કાય / અપૂકાય) and should be preserved verbatim where the native text is quoted; the glossary uses standardized IAST. The web citation above is supplementary, used only to confirm the standard names of the samitis and guptis; the doctrinal content is fully attested within the Jain framework summarized in the tradition knowledge base. The Wikipedia URL was not re-verified live and is provided as a conventional reference only.
